authority.title: Liability Insurance
authority.text_excerpt:
Evidence that a person was or was not insured against liability is not admissible to 
prove whether the person acted negligently or otherwise wrongfully.  But the court 
may admit this evidence for another purpose, such as proving a witness’s bias or 
prejudice or—if controverted—proving agency, ownership, or control.

adversary_models.rebuttal_strategy: Pattern of bias only became clear through cumulative ex parte violations. MCR 2.003(D)(1) allows motion 'as soon as the ground becomes known.'
adversary_models.rebuttal_authority: MCR 2.003(D)(1); Cain v Dep't of Corrections, 451 Mich 470

adversary_models.attack_description: Allegations amount to adverse rulings, not actual bias
adversary_models.rebuttal_strategy: 24 ex parte orders (44% rate), 66 documented ex parte communications, denial of hearings — this exceeds 'adverse rulings' and constitutes structural bias. People v Stevens, 498 Mich 162.
adversary_models.rebuttal_authority: People v Stevens, 498 Mich 162; Armstrong v Ypsilanti Twp, 248 Mich App 573

adversary_models.attack_description: Participation in proceedings constitutes waiver of disqualification
adversary_models.rebuttal_strategy: Self-represented litigant was unaware of right to disqualification. MCR 2.003 does not require pre-emptive withdrawal. Litigant raised concerns at earliest opportunity upon understanding the pattern.
adversary_models.rebuttal_authority: MCR 2.003(D); In re Contempt of Henry, 282 Mich App 656

authority.title: Definitions That Apply to Rules 801–807; Exclusions from Hearsay
authority.text_excerpt:
The following definitions apply under Rules 801–807:  
(a) Statement.  “Statement” means a person’s oral assertion, written assertion, or 
nonverbal conduct if the person intended it as an assertion. 
(b) Declarant.  “Declarant” means the person who made the statement. 
(c) Hearsay.  “Hearsay” means a statement that:  
(1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; 
and 
(2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the 
statement.

authority.title: Definitions That Apply to Rules 801–807; Exclusions from Hearsay
authority.text_excerpt:
The following definitions apply under Rules 801–807:  
(a) Statement.  “Statement” means a person’s oral assertion, written assertion, or 
nonverbal conduct if the person intended it as an assertion. 
(b) Declarant.  “Declarant” means the person who made the statement. 
(c) Hearsay.  “Hearsay” means a statement that:  
(1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; 
and 
(2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the 
statement.

authority.title: NOTICE FOLLOWING INVOLUNTARY TERMINATION OF PARENTAL RIGHTS
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) If the parental rights of a parent whose identity and whereabouts are known are 
involuntarily terminated, the court shall notify the parent, either orally or in a writing, that 
the obligation to support the child will continue until a court of competent jurisdiction 
modifies or terminates the obligation, an order of adoption is entered, or the child is 
emancipated by operation of law. 
(B) If the whereabouts of a parent are unknown, the notice required by subsection (A) may 
be provided

authority.title: TERMINATION OF PARENTAL RIGHTS
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) General.
(1) This rule applies to all proceedings in which termination of parental rights is 
sought. Proceedings for termination of parental rights involving an Indian child, are 
governed by 25 USC 1912 in addition to this rule. 
(2) Parental rights of the respondent over the child may not be terminated unless 
termination was requested in an original, amended, or supplemental petition by:

(b) the child,
(c) the guardian, legal custodian, or representative of the child,

authority.title: FINALIZING ADOPTION; FINDINGS OF COURT
authority.text_excerpt:
Before entering a final order of adoption, the trial court shall determine that the adoptee is 
not the subject of any pending proceedings on rehearing or reconsideration, or on appeal from a 
decision to terminate parental rights. The trial court shall make the following findings on the 
record:
That any appeal of the decision to terminate parental rights has 
reached disposition; that no appeal, application for leave to appeal, 

 
or motion for rehearing or reconsideration is pending; and tha

authority.title: TRANSCRIPTS FOR PURPOSES OF APPEAL
authority.text_excerpt:
In an appeal following the involuntary termination of parental rights, if the court finds that 
the respondent is financially unable to pay for the preparation of transcripts for appeal, the court 
must order transcripts prepared at public expense.

authority.title: PERMANENCY PLANNING HEARINGS
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) Permanency Plan. At or before each permanency planning hearing, the court must 
determine whether the agency has made reasonable efforts to finalize the permanency 
plan. At the hearing, the court must review the permanency plan for a child in foster care. 
The court must determine whether and, if applicable, when:
(1) the child may be returned to the parent, guardian, or legal custodian;
(2) a petition to terminate parental rights should be filed;
(3) the child may be placed in a legal guar

authority.title: POST-TERMINATION REVIEW HEARINGS
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) Review Hearing Requirement. If a child remains in foster care following the 
termination of parental rights to the child, the court must conduct a hearing not more than 
91 days after the termination of parental rights and not later than every 91 days after that 
hearing for the first year following the termination of parental rights to the child. At the 
post-termination review hearing, the court shall review the child's placement in foster care 
and the progress toward the child's adoption

authority.title: REHEARINGS; NEW TRIAL
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) Time and Grounds. Except for the case of a juvenile tried as an adult in the family

authority.title: Definitions That Apply to Rules 801–807; Exclusions from Hearsay
authority.text_excerpt:
The following definitions apply under Rules 801–807:  
(a) Statement.  “Statement” means a person’s oral assertion, written assertion, or 
nonverbal conduct if the person intended it as an assertion. 
(b) Declarant.  “Declarant” means the person who made the statement. 
(c) Hearsay.  “Hearsay” means a statement that:  
(1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; 
and 
(2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the 
statement.

authority.title: The Rule Against Hearsay
authority.text_excerpt: Hearsay is not admissible unless these rules provide otherwise.

authority.title: Residual Exception
authority.text_excerpt:
(a) In General.  Under the following conditions, a hearsay statement is not excluded 
by the rule against hearsay even if the statement is not admissible under a hearsay 
exception in Rule 803 or 804: 
(1) the statement has equivalent circumstantial guarantees of trustworthiness; 
(2) it is offered as evidence of a material fact; 
(3) it is more probative on the point for which it is offered than any other 
evidence that the proponent can obtain through reasonable efforts; and

authority.title: Definitions That Apply to Rules 801–807; Exclusions from Hearsay
authority.text_excerpt:
The following definitions apply under Rules 801–807:  
(a) Statement.  “Statement” means a person’s oral assertion, written assertion, or 
nonverbal conduct if the person intended it as an assertion. 
(b) Declarant.  “Declarant” means the person who made the statement. 
(c) Hearsay.  “Hearsay” means a statement that:  
(1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; 
and 
(2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the 
statement.

authority.text_excerpt:

authority.text_excerpt:
In an appeal following the involuntary termination of parental rights, if the court finds that 
the respondent is financially unable to pay for the preparation of transcripts for appeal, the court 
must order transcripts prepared at public expense.

authority.title: RESTORATION OF APPELLATE RIGHTS.
authority.text_excerpt:
If the defendant, whether convicted by plea or at trial, was denied the right to appellate 
review or the appointment of appellate counsel due to errors by the defendant’s prior attorney or 
the court, or other factors outside the defendant’s control, the trial court shall issue an order 
restarting the time in which to file an appeal or request counsel.

authority.text_excerpt:

Const 1963, art 6, § 30, and further powers provided by Supreme Court rule. Proceedings 
before the commission or a master are governed by these rules. The commission may 
adopt and publish internal operating procedures for its internal operation and the 
administration of its proceedings that do not conflict with this subchapter and shall submit 
them to the Supreme Court for approval.

authority.title: RULES EXCLUSIVE ON DISCIPLINE
authority.text_excerpt:
(A) Proceedings for Discipline. Subchapter 9.100 governs the procedure to discipline 
attorneys. A proceeding under subchapter 9.100 is subject to the superintending control of 
the Supreme Court. An investigation or proceeding may not be held invalid because of a 
nonprejudicial irregularity or an error not resulting in a miscarriage of justice.
(B) Local Bar Associations. A local bar association may not conduct a separate 
proceeding to discipline an attorney.
